expanded_description: Aldehydes are organic compounds containing a terminal carbonyl group ($-\text{CHO}$) where the carbonyl carbon is bonded to at least one hydrogen atom. This section explores aldehyde structure and electronic properties, their distinctive physical properties arising from carbonyl polarity, systematic IUPAC and common nomenclature conventions, and their characteristic reactivity as electrophiles. Aldehydes are among the most reactive carbonyl compounds and serve as crucial intermediates in organic synthesis, biochemistry, and industrial chemistry.

core_concept.principle: The Terminal Carbonyl Group ($-\text{CHO}$) Defines Aldehyde Structure and Reactivity
core_concept.explanation: Aldehydes contain a carbonyl group ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) at the end of a carbon chain, with the carbonyl carbon bonded to one hydrogen and one carbon (or two hydrogens in formaldehyde). The carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry ($\angle \approx 120^\circ$). Oxygen's high electronegativity ($3.5$ vs. carbon's $2.5$) creates a highly polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond with partial positive charge on carbon ($\text{C}^{\delta+}$) and partial negative charge on oxygen ($\text{O}^{\delta-}$). This polarization makes the carbonyl carbon strongly electrophilic, susceptible to nucleophilic attack. The terminal position and presence of the aldehyde hydrogen distinguish aldehydes from ketones and make them more reactive toward oxidation and nucleophilic addition.

image.alt: Diagram showing aldehyde carbonyl group with polar bonds and trigonal planar geometry
image.caption: Aldehyde functional group: terminal $\text{C}=\text{O}$ with $\text{C}^{\delta+}=\text{O}^{\delta-}$ polarization; carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry.

sections.introduction: Aldehydes are organic compounds characterized by a terminal carbonyl functional group ($-\text{CHO}$) in which the carbonyl carbon is bonded to at least one hydrogen atom. The general formula for a simple aldehyde is $\text{R}-\text{CHO}$, where $\text{R}$ represents hydrogen (formaldehyde) or an alkyl/aryl group. Aldehydes are among the most important functional groups in organic chemistry: formaldehyde is used in resins and preservation; acetaldehyde is a metabolic intermediate; benzaldehyde provides almond flavor; glucose and other sugars contain aldehyde groups in their open-chain forms. Understanding aldehyde structure and properties is essential for organic synthesis, biochemistry, and industrial applications.

key_concept.definition: An aldehyde is an organic compound containing a terminal carbonyl group ($-\text{CHO}$) where the carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized and bonded to one hydrogen atom and one carbon group (or two hydrogens in formaldehyde). The general formula is $\text{R}-\text{CHO}$ for most aldehydes.
key_concept.context: Aldehydes are distinguished from ketones by the terminal position of the carbonyl group and the presence of an aldehyde hydrogen. This structural difference makes aldehydes more reactive than ketones toward both oxidation and nucleophilic addition. Aldehydes are classified by the nature of the attached group (aliphatic, aromatic) and by chain length.

image.alt: General structure of aldehydes showing R-CHO format
image.caption: Aldehyde general structure: $\text{R}-\text{CHO}$ where $\text{R}$ = H or alkyl/aryl group; carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry.

principles.statement: The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond in aldehydes is highly polar due to oxygen's electronegativity, creating partial positive charge on carbon ($\text{C}^{\delta+}$) that makes it strongly electrophilic and susceptible to nucleophilic attack.
principles.application: This polarization explains aldehyde reactivity: nucleophiles (hydride, Grignard reagents, cyanide, amines) attack the electrophilic carbonyl carbon. The reactivity order is: formaldehyde > aliphatic aldehydes > aromatic aldehydes > ketones, reflecting both electronic and steric factors.
principles.analogy: Think of the carbonyl carbon as a 'magnet' for electron-rich species: the partial positive charge ($\delta+$) attracts nucleophiles like a magnet attracts iron. The more exposed the carbon (less steric hindrance), the stronger the 'magnetic pull'.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde ($\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$) reacts with $\text{NaBH}_4$ (hydride source) to form ethanol. The hydride ($\text{H}^-$) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, followed by protonation to give the alcohol.

principles.statement: The terminal position of the aldehyde carbonyl, with an attached hydrogen atom, makes aldehydes susceptible to oxidation to carboxylic acids, distinguishing them from ketones.
principles.application: This property enables chemical tests to distinguish aldehydes from ketones: Tollens' reagent (silver mirror test), Fehling's solution (red precipitate), and Benedict's solution all oxidize aldehydes but not ketones. This is also why aldehydes must be protected during synthesis when oxidation-sensitive conditions are used.
principles.analogy: The aldehyde hydrogen is like a 'handle' that oxidizing agents can grab onto. Ketones lack this handle (no H on carbonyl carbon), so they resist oxidation under the same conditions.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde + $\text{KMnO}_4$ (oxidizing agent) $\rightarrow$ acetic acid. The aldehyde hydrogen is replaced by $\text{OH}$, converting $-\text{CHO}$ to $-\text{COOH}$. Ketones like acetone do not react under these mild conditions.

components.definition: The aldehyde functional group consists of an $sp^2$ hybridized carbonyl carbon bonded to oxygen via a double bond ($\text{C}=\text{O}$), to hydrogen via a single bond, and to one carbon group (or H in formaldehyde). The carbonyl group is planar with bond angles $\approx 120^\circ$.
components.interpretation: The trigonal planar geometry and $\pi$-bond create a rigid, flat structure around the carbonyl. The polarized $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond ($\text{C}^{\delta+}=\text{O}^{\delta-}$) is the reactive site for nucleophilic addition; the aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond is the site for oxidation.

image.alt: Diagram showing aldehyde bond angles and lengths
image.caption: Aldehyde structure: $sp^2$ carbonyl carbon with $\approx 120^\circ$ bond angles; polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond ($123 \text{ pm}$) and aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond.

sections.introduction: Aldehydes can be classified in multiple ways: by the nature of the attached group (aliphatic, aromatic), by the number of aldehyde groups (monoaldehydes, dialdehydes), and by chain length and branching. These classifications predict physical properties, reactivity patterns, and appropriate nomenclature conventions.

classifications.type: By Attached Group Type: Aliphatic vs. Aromatic
classifications.value: Classified by whether the group attached to carbonyl is alkyl (aliphatic) or aryl (aromatic)
characteristics: Aromatic aldehydes have reduced carbonyl reactivity due to resonance
Aliphatic aldehydes are generally more reactive toward nucleophiles
classifications.behavior: Aliphatic aldehydes undergo nucleophilic addition readily. Aromatic aldehydes have the carbonyl $\pi$-system conjugated with the aromatic ring, which delocalizes the partial positive charge and reduces electrophilicity. Aromatic aldehydes also resist oxidation more than aliphatic aldehydes.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$: methyl group attached to carbonyl. Highly reactive; oxidizes easily to acetic acid. Used in acetic acid production and as chemical intermediate. $\text{BP} = 20^\circ\text{C}$.

image.caption: Acetaldehyde: simplest aliphatic aldehyde with methyl group attached to carbonyl.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CHO}$: phenyl group attached to carbonyl. Carbonyl conjugated with aromatic ring; less reactive than aliphatic aldehydes. Almond flavor; used in fragrance and synthesis. $\text{BP} = 178^\circ\text{C}$.

classifications.value: Classified by the number of $-\text{CHO}$ groups in the molecule
characteristics: Polyaldehydes: multiple $-\text{CHO}$ groups (e.g., sugars in open-chain form)
More aldehyde groups = higher reactivity and water solubility
classifications.behavior: Each additional aldehyde group increases electrophilic sites and water solubility. Dialdehydes like glutaraldehyde are used as crosslinking agents and disinfectants due to their ability to react with multiple nucleophilic sites on proteins.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$: one aldehyde group. Typical aldehyde reactivity. Used in plastic production and as chemical intermediate. $\text{BP} = 49^\circ\text{C}$.
$\text{OHC}-\text{CHO}$: two aldehyde groups on adjacent carbons. Highly reactive; used in textile finishing and as crosslinking agent. $\text{BP} = 51^\circ\text{C}$.
$\text{OHC}-(\text{CH}_2)_3-\text{CHO}$: five-carbon dialdehyde. Used as disinfectant and tissue fixative in electron microscopy due to protein crosslinking ability.

classifications.value: Classified by the length and structure of the carbon chain attached to carbonyl
characteristics: Medium-chain ($\text{C}_5$-$\text{C}_{10}$): less volatile, used in fragrances
Long-chain ($\text{C}_{11}+$): waxy solids, low water solubility
Branched chains: lower boiling points than straight-chain isomers
classifications.behavior: Short-chain aldehydes are gases or volatile liquids with strong odors (formaldehyde: pungent; acetaldehyde: fruity). Medium-chain aldehydes have pleasant odors used in perfumery (hexanal: grassy; octanal: citrus). Long-chain aldehydes are waxy solids with faint odors.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCHO}$: simplest aldehyde, gas at room temperature. Used in resins, preservation, disinfection. Aqueous solution = formalin ($37\%$ formaldehyde). Highly reactive and toxic.

image.caption: Formaldehyde: simplest aldehyde with two hydrogens on carbonyl carbon.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_4\text{CHO}$: six-carbon aldehyde. Grassy, fruity odor; used in flavorings and fragrances. $\text{BP} = 131^\circ\text{C}$.

sections.key_concept: Aldehyde classification: by attached group (aliphatic vs. aromatic), by number of $-\text{CHO}$ groups (mono-, di-, polyaldehydes), and by chain length (short, medium, long). Classification predicts reactivity, odor, and physical properties.

sections.section_title: IUPAC and Common Nomenclature for Aldehydes
sections.introduction: Aldehydes can be named using systematic IUPAC rules or retained common names. The IUPAC system clearly indicates the aldehyde position and carbon skeleton structure, while common names (often derived from corresponding carboxylic acids) remain widely used in chemical literature and industry. Understanding both systems is essential for reading chemical literature.

key_concept.definition: IUPAC aldehyde names replace the '-e' from the parent alkane with '-al' (e.g., ethanal from ethane). The carbonyl carbon is always position 1, so no position number is needed for simple aldehydes. Common names are derived from corresponding carboxylic acids (formaldehyde from formic acid, acetaldehyde from acetic acid).
key_concept.context: For aldehydes attached to rings, the suffix '-carbaldehyde' is used (e.g., cyclohexanecarbaldehyde). When the aldehyde group is not the principal functional group, the prefix 'oxo-' or 'formyl-' is used. Aromatic aldehydes often retain common names (benzaldehyde).

image.alt: Diagram showing aldehyde naming format with examples
image.caption: Aldehyde naming: IUPAC '-al' suffix (ethanal) vs. common names (acetaldehyde); carbonyl carbon is always position 1.

principles.statement: For IUPAC names, identify the longest carbon chain containing the aldehyde group, replace the '-e' from the parent alkane name with '-al', and number the chain starting from the carbonyl carbon (position 1).
principles.application: This system provides unambiguous names for all aldehydes. The carbonyl carbon is automatically position 1, so no locant is needed for simple aldehydes. For substituted aldehydes, number to give substituents the lowest possible positions.
principles.analogy: Think of the aldehyde carbon as the 'head' of the molecule: it always gets position 1, and you count from there. The '-al' suffix is like a badge that says 'aldehyde here at the end!'
principles.example: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$: 3 carbons = propane $\rightarrow$ propanal. $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$: 4 carbons = butane $\rightarrow$ butanal. $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{CHO}$: 3-carbon chain with methyl = 2-methylpropanal.

principles.statement: Common aldehyde names are derived from the corresponding carboxylic acid names: replace '-ic acid' or '-oic acid' with '-aldehyde' or use the historical root name.
principles.application: These names are deeply entrenched in chemical literature and industry. Formaldehyde (from formic acid), acetaldehyde (from acetic acid), propionaldehyde (from propionic acid), butyraldehyde (from butyric acid).
principles.analogy: Common aldehyde names are like family nicknames: they're shorter and more familiar than formal names, but everyone in the chemistry 'family' knows what they mean.
principles.example: Formic acid $\rightarrow$ formaldehyde. Acetic acid $\rightarrow$ acetaldehyde. Propionic acid $\rightarrow$ propionaldehyde. Benzoic acid $\rightarrow$ benzaldehyde.

principles.statement: For aldehydes attached to rings, use '-carbaldehyde' suffix for cyclic alkanes. Aromatic aldehydes often use retained common names (benzaldehyde) or systematic names (benzenecarbaldehyde).
principles.application: When the $-\text{CHO}$ group is attached directly to a ring, the ring is the parent and '-carbaldehyde' indicates the aldehyde substituent. For benzene derivatives, 'benzaldehyde' is the preferred IUPAC name.
principles.analogy: Ring-attached aldehydes are like 'guests' on the ring 'host': the '-carbaldehyde' suffix tells you the aldehyde is visiting (attached to) the ring rather than being part of the main chain.
principles.example: Cyclohexane + $-\text{CHO}$ = cyclohexanecarbaldehyde. Benzene + $-\text{CHO}$ = benzaldehyde (preferred) or benzenecarbaldehyde (systematic).

worked_examples.title: Naming a Substituted Aliphatic Aldehyde

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing the aldehyde group.
Number from the aldehyde carbon (always position 1).
Identify substituent position with this numbering.
Assemble the name with substituent position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-methylbutanal. The aldehyde carbon is automatically position 1; methyl substituent is at position 3. Common name: isovaleraldehyde.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Aldehyde carbon is always position 1 in IUPAC naming; no locant needed for the $-\text{CHO}$ group itself.

worked_examples.title: Naming an Aromatic Aldehyde with Substituents
worked_examples.problem: Name: benzene ring with $-\text{CHO}$ at position 1 and $-\text{Cl}$ at position 3.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent: benzene with aldehyde = benzaldehyde.
Number the ring starting at aldehyde carbon (position 1), proceeding to give substituent lowest number.
Assemble name with substituent position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-chlorobenzaldehyde. The aldehyde group automatically defines position 1 in aromatic aldehydes; chloro is at position 3 (meta to aldehyde). Common name: m-chlorobenzaldehyde.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Aromatic aldehydes: aldehyde defines position 1 automatically; number ring to give substituents lowest positions.

worked_examples.title: From Name to Structure: 2,2-Dimethylpropanal

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain from IUPAC name: 'propanal' = 3-carbon chain with aldehyde at C1.
Number the chain from aldehyde carbon.
Add substituents at specified position: two methyl groups at C2.
Add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency.
Verify molecular formula and connectivity.

worked_examples.final_answer: 2,2-Dimethylpropanal (common name: pivaldehyde): propanal parent with two methyl groups at C2. Condensed structure: $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CCHO}$. This is a highly branched aldehyde.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Name-to-structure conversion: parent chain with aldehyde first, then add substituents at specified positions, then saturate with hydrogens.

sections.introduction: Aldehyde physical properties are governed by carbonyl polarity, molecular weight, and the absence of hydrogen bond donation. Understanding these trends enables prediction of boiling points, water solubility, and appropriate handling procedures for different aldehydes.

categories.description: Aldehyde boiling points are intermediate between alkanes and alcohols of similar molecular weight due to dipole-dipole interactions without H-bond donation
categories.detailed_mechanism: The polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond creates dipole-dipole interactions between aldehyde molecules, elevating boiling points above nonpolar alkanes. However, aldehydes cannot donate hydrogen bonds (no $\text{O}-\text{H}$), so their boiling points are lower than isomeric alcohols. Boiling points increase with molecular weight due to increased dispersion forces.

examples.explanation: Propanal ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}$, $\text{BP} = 49^\circ\text{C}$) vs. butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$, $\text{BP} = -0.5^\circ\text{C}$) vs. propanol ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}$, $\text{BP} = 97^\circ\text{C}$). Aldehyde BP is higher than alkane (dipole) but lower than alcohol (H-bonding).

image.alt: Graph comparing aldehyde, alkane, and alcohol boiling points
image.caption: Aldehyde boiling points are intermediate: higher than alkanes (dipole), lower than alcohols (no H-bond donation).

examples.explanation: Formaldehyde ($\text{C}_1$, $\text{BP} = -19^\circ\text{C}$) < Acetaldehyde ($\text{C}_2$, $\text{BP} = 20^\circ\text{C}$) < Propanal ($\text{C}_3$, $\text{BP} = 49^\circ\text{C}$) < Butanal ($\text{C}_4$, $\text{BP} = 75^\circ\text{C}$). Each $\text{CH}_2$ adds $\approx 25-30^\circ\text{C}$.

categories.description: Small aldehydes are water-soluble due to H-bond acceptance by carbonyl oxygen; solubility decreases with increasing hydrocarbon character
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carbonyl oxygen lone pairs can accept hydrogen bonds from water molecules, providing water solubility for small aldehydes. However, the hydrophobic alkyl/aryl groups oppose solvation. Rule of thumb: aldehydes with $\leq 4$ carbons are significantly water-soluble; larger aldehydes show limited solubility.

examples.explanation: Formaldehyde: miscible (forms methylene glycol in water). Acetaldehyde: miscible. Propanal: $20 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Butanal: $7 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Pentanal: $1 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Hexanal: insoluble.

image.alt: Graph of aldehyde water solubility vs. chain length
image.caption: Water solubility decreases as hydrocarbon chain length increases.

examples.explanation: Benzaldehyde: $0.3 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$ (slightly soluble). The large hydrophobic aromatic ring dominates over the polar carbonyl, limiting water solubility despite H-bond acceptance capability.

categories.description: Aldehydes have distinctive, often pungent or pleasant odors that vary with chain length and structure
categories.detailed_mechanism: Low-molecular-weight aldehydes have sharp, pungent odors (formaldehyde: irritating; acetaldehyde: fruity). Medium-chain aldehydes ($\text{C}_6$-$\text{C}_{10}$) have pleasant, fruity or floral odors used in perfumery. Long-chain aldehydes have faint, waxy odors. Aromatic aldehydes often have characteristic aromas (benzaldehyde: almond).

examples.explanation: Formaldehyde: pungent, irritating. Acetaldehyde: fruity, pungent. Hexanal: grassy, green. Octanal: citrus, orange. Decanal: waxy, citrus. Benzaldehyde: almond, cherry.

image.alt: Chart of aldehyde odors by structure
image.caption: Aldehyde odors vary dramatically with structure: pungent (short chain) to pleasant (medium chain) to faint (long chain).

sections.key_concept: Aldehyde physical properties: BP intermediate between alkanes and alcohols (dipole-dipole, no H-bond donation); water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ soluble); distinctive odors (pungent short-chain, pleasant medium-chain, almond for benzaldehyde).

sections.introduction: Aldehydes are among the most reactive carbonyl compounds due to the terminal position of the carbonyl group, minimal steric hindrance, and the presence of an oxidizable aldehyde hydrogen. Understanding aldehyde reactivity patterns is essential for organic synthesis and biochemical transformations.

principles.statement: The electrophilic carbonyl carbon in aldehydes undergoes nucleophilic addition reactions with a wide variety of nucleophiles including hydride, Grignard reagents, cyanide, amines, and alcohols.
principles.application: This is the most important reaction type for aldehydes. Mechanism: nucleophile attacks carbonyl carbon, forming tetrahedral intermediate; protonation yields addition product. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones due to less steric hindrance and greater electrophilicity.
principles.analogy: Think of the carbonyl carbon as a 'target' for nucleophilic 'arrows': the more exposed the target (less steric bulk), the easier it is to hit. Aldehydes have one small H and one R group; ketones have two R groups blocking access.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde + $\text{NaBH}_4$ (hydride source) $\rightarrow$ ethanol. Acetaldehyde + $\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}$ (Grignard) $\rightarrow$ 2-propanol after workup. Acetaldehyde + $\text{HCN}$ $\rightarrow$ cyanohydrin.

principles.statement: Aldehydes are readily oxidized to carboxylic acids by mild oxidizing agents ($\text{KMnO}_4$, $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$, Tollens' reagent, Fehling's solution), distinguishing them from ketones.
principles.application: This property enables chemical tests to identify aldehydes: Tollens' reagent produces silver mirror; Fehling's/Benedict's solution produces red $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ precipitate. In synthesis, aldehydes must be protected from oxidation when using oxidizing conditions.
principles.analogy: The aldehyde hydrogen is like a 'fuse' that oxidizing agents can light: once it's replaced by $\text{OH}$, you get a carboxylic acid. Ketones have no fuse (no H on carbonyl), so they don't 'burn' (oxidize) under the same conditions.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde + $\text{KMnO}_4$ $\rightarrow$ acetic acid. Benzaldehyde + Tollens' reagent ($\text{Ag}^+$) $\rightarrow$ benzoic acid + silver mirror. Ketones (acetone) do not react with Tollens' reagent.

principles.statement: Aldehydes are reduced to primary alcohols by hydride reagents ($\text{NaBH}_4$, $\text{LiAlH}_4$) or catalytic hydrogenation ($\text{H}_2$/catalyst).
principles.application: This is a key synthetic transformation for converting aldehydes to alcohols. $\text{NaBH}_4$ is mild and selective for carbonyls; $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is stronger and reduces other functional groups too. Catalytic hydrogenation reduces $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $\text{C}=\text{C}$.
principles.analogy: Reduction is like 'filling up' the carbonyl: you add hydrogen to both the carbon and oxygen, converting the double bond to single bonds and creating an alcohol.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde + $\text{NaBH}_4$ $\rightarrow$ ethanol. Benzaldehyde + $\text{LiAlH}_4$ $\rightarrow$ benzyl alcohol. Propanal + $\text{H}_2$/Pd $\rightarrow$ 1-propanol.

principles.statement: Aldehydes with $\alpha$-hydrogens undergo base-catalyzed aldol addition followed by dehydration to form $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated aldehydes.
principles.application: This is a fundamental carbon-carbon bond-forming reaction in organic synthesis and biochemistry. Mechanism: enolate formation, nucleophilic attack on another aldehyde, then dehydration. Used to build larger molecules from smaller precursors.
principles.analogy: Aldol condensation is like molecular 'Lego': two aldehyde pieces connect together (via enolate attack), then lose water to lock into a more stable, extended structure.
principles.example: Acetaldehyde + base $\rightarrow$ aldol (3-hydroxybutanal) $\rightarrow$ crotonaldehyde (but-2-enal) after dehydration. This creates a new $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond and extends the carbon chain.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Nucleophilic Addition Product
worked_examples.problem: Predict the product of reaction: propanal + $\text{NaBH}_4$, then $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ workup.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the reaction type: $\text{NaBH}_4$ is a hydride reducing agent; this is nucleophilic addition/reduction.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Reaction: nucleophilic addition of H}^- \text{ to carbonyl}$

step_by_step.explanation: Hydride attacks electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming alkoxide intermediate.
Acidic workup protonates alkoxide to form alcohol.
Identify product: primary alcohol with same carbon skeleton.

worked_examples.final_answer: Product: 1-propanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). Aldehyde reduction with $\text{NaBH}_4$ produces primary alcohols. The carbonyl $\text{C}=\text{O}$ is converted to $\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Aldehyde reduction: $\text{NaBH}_4$ adds hydride to carbonyl carbon; workup protonates oxygen to give primary alcohol.

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Aldehyde from Ketone: Tollens' Test
worked_examples.problem: Explain how Tollens' reagent distinguishes propanal from acetone.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the functional groups: propanal is an aldehyde; acetone is a ketone.
Tollens' reagent contains $\text{Ag}^+$ (ammoniacal silver nitrate), a mild oxidizing agent.
Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylic acids; $\text{Ag}^+$ is reduced to metallic silver.
Ketones lack the aldehyde hydrogen and do not react with Tollens' reagent.

worked_examples.final_answer: Propanal gives a positive Tollens' test (silver mirror forms on test tube); acetone gives no reaction. This distinguishes aldehydes (oxidizable) from ketones (resistant to mild oxidation).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tollens' test: aldehydes oxidize to carboxylic acids, reducing $\text{Ag}^+$ to metallic silver (mirror); ketones do not react.

sections.introduction: Several simple and aromatic aldehydes have major industrial, pharmaceutical, and biological importance. Understanding these common aldehydes provides context for why aldehyde chemistry is practically significant in manufacturing, fragrances, biochemistry, and synthesis.

components.function: Resin production (urea-formaldehyde, phenol-formaldehyde); preservation (formalin); disinfectant; chemical feedstock
components.key_feature: Simplest aldehyde; gas at room temperature ($\text{BP} = -19^\circ\text{C}$); typically used as $37\%$ aqueous solution (formalin); highly reactive and toxic; carcinogenic

components.function: Acetic acid production; pyridine synthesis; flavor and fragrance intermediate; metabolic intermediate (ethanol oxidation)
components.key_feature: Volatile liquid ($\text{BP} = 20^\circ\text{C}$); fruity odor; produced in liver during ethanol metabolism; carcinogenic; reactive toward nucleophiles

components.function: Flavoring (almond, cherry); fragrance component; pharmaceutical intermediate; dye precursor
components.key_feature: Aromatic aldehyde; $\text{BP} = 178^\circ\text{C}$; characteristic almond odor; less reactive than aliphatic aldehydes due to conjugation; oxidizes to benzoic acid in air

components.function: Plastic production; rubber chemicals; pharmaceutical intermediate; solvent
components.key_feature: Three-carbon aldehyde; $\text{BP} = 49^\circ\text{C}$; pungent odor; used in synthesis of alcohols and acids via oxidation/reduction

components.function: Disinfectant; tissue fixative for electron microscopy; crosslinking agent for proteins
components.key_feature: Five-carbon dialdehyde; reacts with amine groups on proteins; bactericidal and virucidal; $\text{BP} = 188^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Vision (photoreceptor in retina); cellular differentiation; immune function
components.key_feature: Biological aldehyde; converts between retinal and retinol (vitamin A); light-induced isomerization triggers vision signal; essential nutrient

sections.introduction: Aldehydes produce characteristic signals in infrared (IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, enabling rapid identification without chemical tests. Learning these spectroscopic 'fingerprints' is essential for structure determination in organic chemistry.

categories.description: Aldehydes show two characteristic IR absorptions: carbonyl stretch and aldehyde C-H stretches
categories.detailed_mechanism: The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch appears at $1720-1740 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (slightly higher than ketones due to less conjugation). The aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ stretch shows two weak bands at $2720 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ and $2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (Fermi resonance), which is diagnostic for aldehydes and distinguishes them from ketones.

examples.explanation: Strong, sharp peak at $1720-1740 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Aliphatic aldehydes: $\sim 1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Aromatic aldehydes: $\sim 1700 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (conjugation lowers frequency).

image.alt: IR spectrum showing aldehyde carbonyl stretch region
image.caption: Aldehyde $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch: strong peak $1720-1740 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.

examples.explanation: Two weak bands at $2720 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ and $2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (Fermi doublet). This is unique to aldehydes and distinguishes them from ketones, which lack the aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond.

categories.description: The aldehyde proton is highly deshielded and appears as a distinctive downfield signal
categories.detailed_mechanism: The aldehyde proton ($-\text{CHO}$) is strongly deshielded by the adjacent carbonyl group and appears at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$, one of the most downfield signals in organic NMR. This is highly diagnostic for aldehydes. The signal is typically a singlet or shows small coupling ($J \approx 1-3 \text{ Hz}$) to adjacent protons.

examples.explanation: Aldehyde $\text{H}$: $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ (singlet or small doublet). This is the most distinctive NMR feature of aldehydes. No other common functional group has protons in this region except carboxylic acids ($\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$).

image.alt: NMR spectrum showing aldehyde proton region
image.caption: Aldehyde proton: highly deshielded signal at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$.

examples.explanation: Protons on carbon adjacent to carbonyl ($\alpha$-protons): $\delta 2.0-2.7 \text{ ppm}$. Deshielded by carbonyl electron-withdrawal but less than aldehyde proton.

categories.description: The aldehyde carbonyl carbon appears at characteristic downfield position
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carbonyl carbon in aldehydes appears at $\delta 190-200 \text{ ppm}$ in $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR, slightly upfield from carboxylic acids and esters but downfield from ketones ($\delta 200-220 \text{ ppm}$). This helps distinguish aldehydes from other carbonyl compounds.

examples.explanation: Aldehyde $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 190-200 \text{ ppm}$. Ketone $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 200-220 \text{ ppm}$. Carboxylic acid $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 170-185 \text{ ppm}$. This range helps identify carbonyl type.

sections.key_concept: IR: $\text{C}=\text{O}$ at $1720-1740 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ doublet at $2720, 2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (diagnostic). $^1\text{H}$ NMR: aldehyde $\text{H}$ at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ (highly deshielded). $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR: carbonyl $\text{C}$ at $\delta 190-200 \text{ ppm}$.

sections.introduction: Aldehyde chemistry presents unique challenges in identification, naming, and reactivity prediction. Understanding common pitfalls helps ensure accurate communication of aldehyde structures and realistic expectations of their behavior in chemical contexts.

categories.description: Both contain carbonyl groups, but aldehydes are terminal ($-\text{CHO}$) while ketones are internal ($-\text{C}(=\text{O})-$)
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students sometimes overlook the aldehyde hydrogen or misidentify the carbonyl position. Aldehydes have at least one H attached to the carbonyl carbon; ketones have two carbon substituents. This structural difference affects reactivity (aldehydes oxidize, ketones don't) and spectroscopy (aldehyde H at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ in NMR).

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Calling $\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3$ an aldehyde. CORRECT: It is acetone (a ketone) because the carbonyl carbon is bonded to two carbons, not to H. Propanal ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$) is an aldehyde with H on carbonyl.

categories.description: Adding position number for aldehyde carbon in IUPAC naming
categories.detailed_mechanism: The aldehyde carbon is always position 1 in IUPAC naming, so specifying '1-' is redundant and incorrect. Students sometimes write 'butan-1-al' instead of 'butanal'.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: butan-1-al (redundant position). CORRECT: butanal (aldehyde carbon is automatically position 1). Position numbers are only needed for substituents on the chain.

categories.description: Assuming aldehydes are stable under oxidizing conditions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Aldehydes are easily oxidized to carboxylic acids by many common oxidizing agents ($\text{KMnO}_4$, $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$, even air over time). Students may plan syntheses without protecting aldehydes from oxidation, leading to unexpected products.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Using $\text{KMnO}_4$ to oxidize an alkene in a molecule that also contains an aldehyde. CORRECT: Protect the aldehyde (as acetal) before oxidation, or use selective oxidizing conditions that don't affect aldehydes.

categories.description: Confusing aldehyde signals with other functional groups in IR or NMR
categories.detailed_mechanism: The aldehyde $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch overlaps with ketone and ester carbonyls in IR. The key distinction is the aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ doublet at $2720, 2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. In NMR, aldehyde protons ($\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$) can be confused with carboxylic acid protons ($\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$), but acids are broader and more downfield.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Identifying a compound as a ketone based solely on $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch at $1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. CORRECT: Check for aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ doublet at $2720, 2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$; if present, it's an aldehyde, not a ketone.

sections.section_title: Aldehyde Nomenclature and Properties Summary Checklist
sections.introduction: A quick-reference summary consolidates aldehyde naming rules and property trends into an actionable checklist for identifying, naming, and predicting behavior of aldehyde compounds.

components.composition: Look for terminal $-\text{CHO}$ with H on carbonyl carbon
components.function: Distinguishes aldehydes from ketones and other carbonyls
components.key_feature: Carbonyl carbon bonded to H and one C (or two H in formaldehyde)

components.composition: Longest chain with $-\text{CHO}$; replace '-e' with '-al'; carbonyl = position 1
components.key_feature: No position number needed for $-\text{CHO}$ itself; number substituents from carbonyl end

components.composition: BP intermediate (dipole, no H-bond donation); water solubility decreases with chain length
components.key_feature: $\leq \text{C}_4$ aldehydes water-soluble; distinctive odors (pungent to pleasant)

components.composition: Electrophilic carbonyl (nucleophilic addition); oxidizable to carboxylic acids
components.function: Informs reaction conditions and synthesis planning
components.key_feature: More reactive than ketones; Tollens'/Fehling's tests distinguish from ketones

components.function: Confirms aldehyde identity in unknown compounds
components.key_feature: Aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ doublet in IR and $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ signal in NMR are diagnostic

summary: Aldehydes are organic compounds containing a terminal carbonyl group ($-\text{CHO}$) where the carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized and bonded to one hydrogen atom. The polarized $\text{C}^{\delta+}=\text{O}^{\delta-}$ bond makes the carbonyl carbon strongly electrophilic, susceptible to nucleophilic addition reactions. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones due to less steric hindrance and greater electrophilicity. Classification: by attached group (aliphatic vs. aromatic), by number of $-\text{CHO}$ groups (mono-, di-, polyaldehydes), and by chain length. IUPAC nomenclature: replace '-e' from parent alkane with '-al' (e.g., ethanal); carbonyl carbon is always position 1 (no locant needed); common names derived from carboxylic acids (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, benzaldehyde). Physical properties: boiling points intermediate between alkanes and alcohols (dipole-dipole, no H-bond donation); water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ soluble); distinctive odors (pungent short-chain, pleasant medium-chain, almond for benzaldehyde). Chemical reactivity: nucleophilic addition (hydride, Grignard, cyanide, amines), oxidation to carboxylic acids (Tollens', Fehling's tests distinguish from ketones), reduction to primary alcohols, aldol condensation (with $\alpha$-hydrogens). Spectroscopic identification: IR shows $\text{C}=\text{O}$ at $1720-1740 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + diagnostic aldehyde $\text{C}-\text{H}$ doublet at $2720, 2820 \text{ cm}^{-1}$; $^1\text{H}$ NMR shows aldehyde proton at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ (highly deshielded); $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR shows carbonyl carbon at $\delta 190-200 \text{ ppm}$. Common aldehydes include formaldehyde (resins, preservation), acetaldehyde (metabolic intermediate, acetic acid production), benzaldehyde (flavoring, fragrance), and glutaraldehyde (disinfectant, crosslinking). Critical considerations: aldehydes oxidize easily (protect during synthesis); aldehyde carbon is always position 1 in naming; spectroscopic fingerprints distinguish from ketones.
